sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Abdominal pain is a very common symptom experienced by patients. In many patients the cause is benign or self-limiting, but some causes are time critical and may require urgent intervention.
- Time critical abdominal emergencies can include:
  - Ectopic pregnancy
  - Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA)
  - Peritonitis and sepsis
  - Testicular or ovarian torsion
  - Uncontrolled GI bleeding
  - Bowel obstruction
  - Acute pancreatitis

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Severe abdominal pain and/or tenderness suggestive of peritonism
- Abdominal distention
- Constipation
- Altered vital signs, fever, diaphoresis, pallor, or signs of sepsis
- Nausea and/or vomiting, haematemesis, melena
- History of haematemesis, melaena, or haematochezia
- Vaginal discharge or bleeding

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Cardiac ischaemia can mimic gastro-oesophageal or upper abdominal pain. Maintain a low threshold for 12 lead ECG in epigastric or upper abdominal pain, especially in older patients or those with cardiac risk factors
- The presence of normal vital signs does not preclude serious abdominal disease
- AAA may masquerade as renal colic or back pain, especially in older patients
- Red flags requiring urgent escalation include haemodynamic instability, rigid abdomen or peritonism, severe sudden-onset pain, gastrointestinal bleeding, persistent vomiting or obstruction, positive pregnancy test with abdominal pain, or known AAA with abdominal or back pain

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Perform repeated vital signs
- Keep the patient at rest and allow a position of comfort
- Perform Secondary Survey
- Abdominal inspection and gentle palpation as tolerated
- Abdominal auscultation
- Consider simple analgesia if appropriate
- Perform urinalysis and pregnancy testing where appropriate and available

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access
- Consider fluid resuscitation if indicated, especially if shock, dehydration, or significant bleeding is suspected
- Consider appropriate analgesia
- Obtain a 12 lead ECG where indicated

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- In ruptured AAA, major GI bleeding, or ruptured ectopic pregnancy, the goal of early fluid therapy is maintenance of vital-organ perfusion rather than normalisation of blood pressure
- Patients with leaking or ruptured AAA are often in severe pain and may require timely opioid analgesia
- Cardiac ischaemia may present with abdominal pain, especially epigastric pain, so ECG use should remain liberal in appropriate patients
- The assessment of abdominal pain is complex and requires multi-modal investigation
- Patient education: explain warning signs that require emergency care and advise follow-up where appropriate
